name: Gastroenteritis
notes: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines; often acute and associated with dietary indiscretion or infection.

name: Pancreatitis
notes: Inflammation of the pancreas; can range from mild to life-threatening and often causes significant abdominal discomfort.

name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
notes: Chronic inflammation of the intestines, typically causing long-term digestive signs and weight loss.

name: Intestinal Parasites
notes: Parasitic infections such as roundworms, hookworms, or whip-worms affecting the intestinal tract.

name: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus
notes: Also known as bloat; a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention.

name: Foreign Body Obstruction
notes: Ingestion of an object that becomes lodged in the gastrointestinal tract, potentially requiring surgery.

name: Chronic Hepatitis
notes: Long-term inflammation of the liver that can progress to liver failure if untreated.

name: Cholestasis / Bile Duct Obstruction
notes: Reduced or blocked bile flow from the liver; may be caused by stones, inflammation, or tumors.

name: Portosystemic Shunt
notes: Abnormal blood vessel shunts blood around the liver, reducing detoxification and causing neurologic signs.

name: Liver Neoplasia
notes: Primary or metastatic tumors affecting the liver; signs are often chronic and nonspecific.

name: Hypothyroidism
notes: Common in middle-aged dogs. Often presents with chronic low energy and gradual weight gain despite no change in diet.

name: Diabetes Mellitus
notes: Characterized by persistent high blood sugar. Classic pattern is increased thirst, increased urination, and weight loss.

name: Cushing's Disease (Hyperadrenocorticism)
notes: Caused by chronic excess cortisol. Often presents with a pot-bellied abdomen, panting, and increased thirst/urination.

name: Addison's Disease (Hypoadrenocorticism)
notes: Often has vague, intermittent signs like GI upset and low energy that can suddenly progress to collapse during an Addisonian crisis.

name: Obesity
notes: Chronic condition that increases risk of diabetes, joint disease, and cardiovascular stress.

name: Hypothyroidism
notes: Chronic thyroid hormone deficiency. Often seen in middle-aged or older dogs and can present with gradual changes in weight, coat quality, and activity level. Always consult a veterinarian for diagnosis.

name: Diabetes Mellitus
notes: Metabolic disease where the body cannot properly regulate blood sugar. Common early signs include drinking and urinating more than usual, weight loss, and sometimes urinary accidents. Requires veterinary management.

name: Cushing's Disease (Hyperadrenocorticism)
notes: Condition caused by chronically elevated cortisol levels. Dogs often show increased appetite, thirst, panting, a rounded abdomen, and gradual coat or skin changes. Evaluation by a veterinarian is required.

name: Addison's Disease (Hypoadrenocorticism)
notes: Adrenal gland insufficiency that can cause vague chronic signs or sudden collapse. Because it can be life-threatening in crisis situations, any dog with these signs should be seen by a veterinarian urgently.

name: Obesity / Overweight
notes: Excess body weight that increases the risk of joint disease, diabetes, and heart strain. Often develops gradually. Owners should discuss safe weight-loss plans with a veterinarian.

name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
recommendedActions: Seek veterinary evaluation as soon as back or neck pain is noticed
Strict rest and activity restriction
Emergency care if paralysis or loss of bladder/bowel control develops

name: Idiopathic Epilepsy
riskFactors: Onset between 1 and 5 years of age
recommendedActions: Track seizure frequency and duration in logs
See a veterinarian to rule out structural or metabolic causes
Follow long-term anti-seizure medication plan if prescribed

name: Vestibular Disease
recommendedActions: Seek veterinary evaluation promptly
Provide non-slip surfaces and assist with mobility
Monitor for progression or improvement over several days

name: Degenerative Myelopathy
recommendedActions: Discuss genetic testing with a veterinarian for at-risk breeds
Implement physical therapy and weight management
Adapt the home environment for mobility support

name: Osteoarthritis (Degenerative Joint Disease)
recommendedActions: Maintain lean body weight
Use joint supplements or medications as prescribed
Provide non-slip flooring and comfortable bedding
Adjust exercise intensity rather than stopping activity entirely

name: Hip Dysplasia
recommendedActions: Screen at-risk breeds with hip radiographs
Implement weight management and controlled exercise
Discuss surgical and medical management options with a veterinarian

name: Cranial Cruciate Ligament Rupture
symptoms: Pain on manipulation of the knee
recommendedActions: Seek veterinary evaluation as soon as acute lameness appears
Restrict activity until diagnosis is confirmed
Discuss surgical repair and rehabilitation plans

name: Medial Luxating Patella
symptoms: Intermittent skipping or hopping on a hind leg
recommendedActions: Monitor mild cases for progression
Maintain ideal body weight
Seek orthopedic consultation for moderate to severe cases
